question: Q2: Integrating AI Without Losing Quality

sections.model: Where AI Fits in Your Dev Loop
sections.prompt: Raise your hand if your team has mapped where AI does and doesn’t fit (plan → code → review → test → release).
sections.hint: Examples: drafting code/tests, refactors, docs, exploratory design; places to avoid: security-critical code, payment flows.
no_hands: If you started small, what’s the safest first step (e.g., tests, docs, boilerplate)?
Which parts of Flutter work feel too risky or fiddly for AI right now?
What signal would tell you it’s time to expand AI’s role?
hands_up: Walk us through your loop—where AI sits, and where it’s off-limits.
How do you keep flow without letting AI over-edit?
Any roles AI performs better than humans (and vice versa)?
What changed in your PR size/shape when AI joined the loop?
follow_ups: Do you switch AI off for certain modules (auth, billing, unsafe APIs)?
Anyone use AI-free spikes to reason about hard problems first?

sections.prompt: Raise your hand if you use explicit safeguards for AI-suggested code.
sections.hint: Examples: tests before merges, static analysis, duplication checks, mandatory reviews.
no_hands: Which one or two guardrails would give you 80% of the safety with minimal overhead?
How could you start measuring the rework/debug time from AI output?
Who needs to sign off before shipping AI-touched code?
hands_up: What guardrails actually catch the most issues (tests, SAST, type checks, canary releases)?
Do you require a human reviewer for all AI-touched files?
How do you document AI involvement (PR labels, commit trailers)?
Have you tuned prompts/checklists to reduce false positives?
follow_ups: A small test suite or a strict code review—what saves more time in practice?
Anyone tried gating merges on coverage or security scans for AI-changed files?

sections.prompt: Raise your hand if you have guidance for juniors and seniors on when to lean on AI.
sections.hint: Examples: learning goals, task selection, pairing patterns, code reading first, prompt hygiene.
no_hands: What would a simple growth-first policy look like for juniors using AI?
How do you keep architectural thinking sharp when AI drafts code?
What practice helps you verify AI output quickly (reading diffs, micro-benchmarks)?
hands_up: How do seniors use AI without hollowing out review quality?
What tasks do juniors do without AI to build fundamentals?
Favorite prompt patterns that teach—not just paste code?
Any evidence your team’s quality improved, not just speed?
follow_ups: When do you ask teammates instead of the model?
One habit that made you less dependent on AI over time?

sections.model: Team Norms, Clients, and Production Expectations
sections.prompt: Raise your hand if you have a written policy for AI in client or production work.
sections.hint: Examples: privacy, data handling, attribution, model choice, incident response.
no_hands: What minimum policy would make clients comfortable (two or three clear rules)?
Where would you state it—SOW, README, CONTRIBUTING, or CI checks?
How do you handle third-party code or license questions from AI output?
hands_up: What’s in your policy—where AI is allowed, reviewer requirements, secrets handling?
Any client conversations that changed your boundaries?
How do you log/trace AI involvement for accountability?
Did your policy reduce regressions or review time?
follow_ups: Do you keep an AI exceptions list (cases where AI is off-limits)?
Anyone set per-repo model/assistant defaults to avoid tool sprawl?

wrap_up: Share one golden rule you use to keep quality high with AI in the loop.
One scenario where you deliberately turn AI off—what’s the tell?
A change you’ll try next sprint (guardrail, checklist, or workflow tweak).
